block:
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    Fixed Point Formats (Q-Format): 16-bit Example           |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

  Format: Q8.8 (8 bits Integer, 8 bits Fraction)
           |
         [MSB]                               [LSB]
           V                                   V
   [ Sign ][ 7-bit Integer ] . [ 8-bit Fraction ]
           \_______________/ ^ \________________/
            Range: -128~127  |  Precision: 1/256
                             |
                   Virtual Decimal Point
                 (Fixed permanently by logic)

  * Rule: The ALU treats this strictly as a 16-bit
    Integer. The programmer or compiler manages the dot.
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    Multiplication Overhead: Fixed-Point vs Floating-Point   |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

  [ Fixed-Point Multiplication (Q8.8 * Q8.8) ]
   1. Normal Integer MULTIPLY (1 cycle)
       Result dynamically expands to 32 bits (Q16.16)
   2. Arithmetic SHIFT RIGHT 8 times (1 cycle)
       Brings the decimal point back to Q8.8 format.
   [Total: 2 cycles] !! Breathtaking Speed !!

  [ Floating-Point Multiplication (IEEE 754) ]
   1. Extract Signs and XOR them.
   2. Extract Exponents, Add them, Subtract Bias.
   3. Extract Mantissas, Add hidden '1'.
   4. Multiply Mantissas (slow).
   5. Normalize Result (Shift left/right).
   6. Rounding logic pass.
   [Total: 10~15+ cycles] !! Pipeline Killer !!
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    The Hamming Radar Mesh: Locating the Crime Scene         |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

  [ Payload (4 bits): 1 0 1 1 ]
  Rule: Place Parity Bits (P) at powers of 2 (positions 1, 2, 4).
  String Setup:  P1  P2  D1  P4  D2  D3  D4
  Index Number:   1   2   3   4   5   6   7

  [ The Intersecting Radar Logic (Even Parity Coverage) ]
  P1 covers skipping every 1_bit: positions 1, 3, 5, 7.
  P2 covers skipping every 2_bits: positions 2, 3, 6, 7.
  P4 covers skipping every 4_bits: positions 4, 5, 6, 7.

  * The Genius Assembly: 
    Let's say bit 6 (D3) gets struck by Cosmic Radio noise!
    Bit 6 is part of which radar coverage?
    Look at the binary of 6: 0 1 1 0  (4 + 2).
    It is covered by P4 and P2 cameras!
  
  [ Receiver Error Checker (Syndrome Calculus) ]
  Receiver gets the noisy packet. Checks Radars:
  P1 check: OK! (Outputs 0)
  P2 check: FAIL. (Outputs 1)
  P4 check: FAIL. (Outputs 1)
  
  * Math Magic (Syndrome Vector): P4(1) P2(1) P1(0) = 110 (Binary).
  What is 110 in Decimal? It is EXACTLY 6 !!!
  The hardware shouts: "The 6th bit is fraudulent! Invert it!"
  CPU flips bit 6 back. Data is 100% saved without asking for a resend!
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    The Double-Bit Trap: Hamming Code's Nightmare            |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

  [ Original Safe Transmission ]
  Valid Hamming Frame sent -> 1 0 1 1 0 1 0

  [ The Double Lightning Burst Strike! ]
  Noise flips BOTH Bit 3 (Data) and Bit 5 (Data)!
  Received Frame: 1 0 [0] 1 [1] 1 0  (Two corruptions)

  [ Syndrome Radar Confusion Math ]
  P1 Check covers: 1, 3, 5, 7.
  - Bit 3 AND 5 both flipped. Two flips cancel each other out in XOR!
  - P1 outputs: OK (0) !  <-- BLIND!!

  P2 Check covers: 2, 3, 6, 7.
  - Only Bit 3 flipped here.
  - P2 outputs: ERROR (1) !

  P4 Check covers: 4, 5, 6, 7.
  - Only Bit 5 flipped here.
  - P4 outputs: ERROR (1) !

  [ The Disastrous Action (Syndrome = 110) ]
  Syndrome Vector Vector: P4(1) P2(1) P1(0) = Integer 6 !!!
  The hardware shouts: "Bit 6 is corrupt! INVERT IT!"
  * Hardware blindly flips bit 6 (which was perfectly INNOCENT).
  
  [ Result: System Decimation ]
  Now bits 3, 5, AND 6 are all corrupted! The system just added
  a THIRD error while trying to fix the two, creating irreversible 
  garbage data! Memory crashes fatally!
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    Modulo-2 Division: The Genius XOR Hardware Math          |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

  [ Setup for CRC ]
  Message (M): 1010
  Generator Key (G): 1011 (Degree 3, so add 3 zero-paddings to M)
  Padded Message: 1010'000

  [ Hardware Polynomial Division (Modulo-2 / XOR) ]
               1 0 0 1  (Quotient - Toss it away)
         _____________
   1011 ) 1010000
          1011
          ---- (XOR subtraction: NO BORROW, NO CARRY)
            0100
            0000
            ----
              1000
              1011
              ----
               011  <- REMAINDER (This is the CRC!)

  [ The Perfect Transmission ]
  Transmitted Frame = Original M + CRC  =>  1010'011

  [ Receiver Validation ]
  Divide the ENTIRE received frame (1010011) by same G (1011).
  If division REMAINDER == 000, then Packet is PERFECT.
  If noise flips even a single bit (or 10 bits), the remainder 
  will explode into non-zeros. Packet Dropped instantly!
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    The Mathematics of Undefeated Burst Protection           |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

  [ The Polynomial Trap (G) ]
  Suppose Generator G(x) has a degree of 32 (like Ethernet CRC-32).
  Length of the remainder = exactly 32 bits.

  [ Scenario: Massive Radio Noise Hits the Wire ]
  A burst of noise destroys a contiguous chunk of 25 bits 
  in the middle of a 1500-byte frame.
  
  Checksum Response: "Maybe the messed up bits canceled each other out 
  to the exact same sum! Pass." (Danger!)

  CRC Mathematical Response: 
  The corrupted frame is interpreted mathematically as:
  Received Frame = Original_Polynomial / G(x) + Error_Burst_Polynomial / G(x)
  
  Theorem 1: If the length of the Burst Error (25 bits) is LESS than 
  or EQUAL to the degree of G(x) (32 bits), it is mathematically 
  IMPOSSIBLE for the Error Polynomial to be cleanly divisible by G(x) 
  without a remainder!
  
  Result: Remainder will NEVER equal 0. 
  Conclusion: CRC detects 100% of ALL burst errors <= 32 bits long.
  (And 99.999999% of burst errors > 32 bits long).
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    The Geometrical Cube of Hamming Distance Vectors         |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

  [ A 3-Bit Data World (Cube coordinates x, y, z) ]
  Suppose we ONLY allow two valid messages: 
  Message A: 000  (All clear)
  Message B: 111  (Fire Missile)

  Hamming Distance between A & B = 3  (Every bit is different)

  [ Scenario: Cosmic Ray flips ONE bit of 000 ]
  Sent: 000
  Received: 001  (A 1-bit Error!)

  [ The Receiver's Geometry Brain (Correction Logic) ]
  The receiver plots "001" on the 3D Cube.
  "Distance to 000? It's exactly 1 step away."
  "Distance to 111? It's 2 steps away (101 -> 111)."
  
  * Conclusion: "001" is GEOMETRICALLY CLOSEST to "000".
    The receiver safely AUTOCORRECTS the data back to "000".

  [ Formula: Error Capability ]
  Minimum Hamming Distance required = D(min)
  - To DETECT an error of 'e' bits:  D(min) = e + 1
  - To CORRECT an error of 'c' bits: D(min) = 2c + 1
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    The Parity Bit Vulnerability: Proved by Hamming          |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

  [ Valid Even Parity Codes (Length 3 + 1 bit) ]
  Data A: 000 [0]  (Distance between A and B?)
  Data B: 001 [1]  -> Differ at 3rd bit & Parity bit. (Distance = 2)
  Data C: 011 [0]  -> Differ at 2nd bit & Parity bit. (Distance = 2)
  
  * Conclusion: Every Valid Even Parity Code is EXACTLY 
    a Minimum Hamming Distance (D_min) of TWO apart.
    D(min) = 2.

  [ The Hamming Threshold Test for Parity Bit ]
  1. Detect 'e' errors: D(min) >= e + 1
     Substitute D(min) = 2.
     2 >= e + 1  =>  e = 1.
     * Proof: Parity can ONLY DETECT up to 1 bit error safely!

  2. Correct 'c' errors: D(min) >= 2c + 1
     Substitute D(min) = 2.
     2 >= 2c + 1  =>  c = 0.5 (Impossible half-bit).
     * Proof: Parity CAN NEVER CORRECT even a single bit error! 
       (Because the corrupted bit is equally far from two valid points!)
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    The Elegant ASCII 7-Bit Architecture Table Map           |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

  Total Space: 2^7 = 128 codes (from 0 to 127)

  [ Block 1: The Control Gods (0 ~ 31, and 127) ]
  Non-printable characters strictly for Machine Control.
  0 (NUL): Null character (Ends C-Strings natively!)
  7 (BEL): Rings the physical bell on the terminal.
  10 (LF): Line Feed (Go to next line)
  13 (CR): Carriage Return (Move cursor to start of line)

  [ Block 2: Symbols and Digits (32 ~ 64) ]
  32 (Space), 48~57 (Numbers '0' to '9')
  * Hack: Number '0' is 48 (0011 0000). 
    To convert char '5' to int 5, just subtract 48 (or mask)!

  [ Block 3: The Alphabet Core (65 ~ 122) ]
  65 ('A'): 0100 0001    |  97 ('a'): 0110 0001
  66 ('B'): 0100 0010    |  98 ('b'): 0110 0010
  
  * The Genius Math: 'a' (97) - 'A' (65) = EXACTLY 32.
    Bit 5 (Value 32) acts as an upper/lowercase toggle switch!
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    The PASCAL vs C String Architecture Battle (Memory MAP)  |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

  Saving the word "HELLO" in RAM.

  [ Pascal String (Length Prefix) ]
  Address 0: [ 5 ] (Length of string is 5)
  Address 1: [ H ] Address 2: [ E ] Address 3: [ L ]
  Address 4: [ L ] Address 5: [ O ]
  * Problem: Needs metadata (length) upfront, limiting size
    (e.g., if length is 1 byte, max string is 255 chars).

  [ C String (ASCII NUL termination) ]
  Address 0: [ H ] Address 1: [ E ] Address 2: [ L ]
  Address 3: [ L ] Address 4: [ O ] Address 5: [ \0 ]
  
  * Assembly while-loop execution:
    mov al, [rsi]    ; Load ASCII char
    test al, al      ; AND it with itself. If '0', Zero Flag sets!
    jz _end_string   ; Hardware jumps instantly to end !!

  * The Genius: CPU ALU treats ASCII "0000 0000" as the
    literal integer '0'. CPU Branch-Predictors love checking 0.
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    The Transient Physical Error: Binary vs Gray Code        |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
  [ Moving from Angle 3 to Angle 4 on a Robot Arm ]

  1. Pure Binary Encoder (Catastrophe Zone)
     Position 3:  0 1 1 
                      |   <-- (Sensors flip state!)
     Position 4:  1 0 0 
     
     * What if the middle bit flips 1 nanosecond late?
     Read exactly at the boundary: 1 1 0  (Wait, this is 6 !!)
     Robot reads "Position 6" and violently jerks backwards!

  2. Gray Code Encoder (Safe Zone)
     Position 3:  0 1 0 
                      |   <-- (ONLY the left bit flips!)
     Position 4:  1 1 0 
     
     * Read exactly at the boundary:
     It's either 010 (Position 3) OR 110 (Position 4).
     There is NO impossible garbage value generated!
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    The Hardware Translator: Binary to Gray (and back)       |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
  [ Binary to Gray Conversion (Encode to send to Sensor) ]
  Rule: G(n) = B(n) XOR B(n+1)
  
  Binary  (B):   1     0     1     0   (Value 10)
                 |   / |   / |   / | 
               (XOR) (XOR) (XOR)   |
                 V     V     V     V
  Gray    (G):   1     1     1     1   (Gray Code 10)
  
  * Blazing Fast: All XOR gates run simultaneously (Parallel).
    Delay is only 1 gate logic propagation (Nano-seconds).

  [ Gray to Binary Conversion (Decode to read in CPU) ]
  Rule: B(n) = G(n) XOR B(n+1) [Recursive!]
  
  Gray    (G):   1     1     1     1 
                 |     |     |     |
            (+)->|->(XOR)->(XOR)->(XOR)   (Cascade Ripple)
                 V     V     V     V
  Binary  (B):   1     0     1     0
  
  * CPU Penalty: Gray-to-Binary is SEQUENTIAL. You must wait
    for the first bit to resolve before XORing the next bit.
    Creates a "Ripple Delay" in hardware pipelines.
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|    Sign-Magnitude Addition Logic Flow: A + B                |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+

  START  [ A + B ]
    |
    +--> Q1: Are the signs of A and B the same?
          |
          +-- (YES) --> ADD magnitudes. Keep the original
          |             sign bit.
          |
          +-- (NO)  --> Q2: Which magnitude is larger?
                         |
                         +--> Subtract smaller from larger.
                              Attach the sign of the larger.

  Result: One simple ADD instruction requires multiple
          hardware branches (Comparisons & Subtractions).
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
